sections.title: Overview
sections.content: High-risk mental health presentations involve imminent risk of harm to self or others, severe behavioural disturbance, loss of reality testing, or inability to care for basic needs. Examples include suicidal crisis, severe agitation, psychosis, violent behaviour, or severe self-neglect.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Suicidal ideation, recent self-harm, or suicide attempt
- Severe agitation, aggression, paranoia, or psychosis
- Marked disorganisation, bizarre behaviour, or loss of insight
- Catatonia or profound withdrawal
- Inability to maintain basic safety or self-care

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Personal safety and bystander safety come first
- Assess for weapons, escalating behaviour, and need for police or other agencies
- Always consider organic causes of behavioural disturbance such as hypoxia, hypoglycaemia, intoxication, overdose, TBI, seizure, or stroke
- Maintain dignity, privacy, and the least restrictive approach possible
- Sedation and restraint are high risk and should be last-resort, proportionate, and carefully documented
- Positive RASS alone does not mandate sedation

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Ensure safety and remove hazards where possible
- Primary Survey, including BGL and SpO2 where possible
- Use calm, non-confrontational de-escalation and clear communication
- Conduct frequent RASS assessment where relevant
- Restrain only if essential to protect the patient or others, using the minimum force necessary
- If suicide risk is identified, do not leave the patient alone
- Seek early support from the on-call doctor and relevant mental health pathways

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Address organic causes as soon as practicable
- Sedation must be discussed with the on-call doctor and should follow failed de-escalation, clear risk, and careful reassessment
- Pharmacologic options should be guided by local protocol, clinician skill, age, comorbidity, and pregnancy status
- Monitor closely after any sedation, including airway, breathing, circulation, and level of consciousness

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Debrief and staff support may be appropriate after significant incidents
- Documentation should clearly record risk, capacity, de-escalation attempts, sedation rationale, and any restraint used
